research_topic: Tauri+React+ReactFlow failure cases, limitations, and rejected alternative re-evaluation

tauri_failure_cases.summary: No major project publicly abandoned Tauri for Electron. Trend is overwhelmingly Electron->Tauri migration. However, significant platform-specific issues exist.

findings.issue: Windows WebView2 spawn hanging in production
findings.detail: Command.spawn() intermittently hangs in production on Windows. Reported Oct 2024, affects shell plugin reliability.
findings.relevance: Direct impact on CLI tool integration (claude-code sidecar). If spawn hangs, the core AI interaction breaks.

findings.issue: Cannot kill child processes on Windows
findings.detail: child.kill() does not terminate spawned processes on Windows. PyInstaller binaries and general subprocess cleanup affected. Issue persists since 2022.
findings.relevance: Critical for sidecar lifecycle management. If claude-code process cannot be killed, orphan processes accumulate.

findings.detail: Even with correct tauri.conf.json config, shell:execute throws 'program not allowed' errors. Feb 2025.
findings.relevance: Could block sidecar integration during development and confuse debugging.

findings.detail: Different WebView engines per OS (WebView2/Windows, WebKitGTK/Linux, WebKit/macOS) cause layout differences, CSS quirks, and feature gaps. SVG/PDF rendering bugs on Mac. WebKitGTK reported increasingly unstable.
findings.relevance: UI must be tested on all 3 platforms. Styling that works on Windows may break on macOS/Linux.

findings.issue: WebView2 fails under Windows Administrator Protection
findings.detail: On Windows 11 Insider builds with Administrator Protection, WebView2 cannot start due to user data directory access issues.
findings.relevance: Edge case but could affect users running as admin on newer Windows 11 builds.

findings.detail: Frontend checks fail on some Intel Macs. Form controls broken. State-based forms not updating.
findings.relevance: Shrinking Intel Mac user base, but still relevant for older hardware.

findings.detail: Remote iFrames could access Tauri IPC endpoints. Fixed in Tauri 2.0.0-beta.20+. Requires XSS in iFrame to exploit.
findings.relevance: Low risk for our use case (no untrusted iFrames), but shows Tauri's security model has had vulnerabilities.

tauri_failure_cases.mitigation_assessment: The Windows process kill issue is the most concerning for our sidecar use case. Workaround: use IPC via localhost HTTP server instead of stdin/stdout, or implement custom process group killing via Rust command. The WebView inconsistency issue is manageable with thorough cross-platform testing and CSS variable usage.

reactflow_limitations.summary: ReactFlow has a documented ~1000 node ceiling due to DOM-based rendering. For a learning knowledge graph, this is unlikely to be hit but must be monitored.

findings.detail: ReactFlow maintainers explicitly state it is not made for 1000+ nodes. Performance degrades with complex custom nodes. At 10k nodes, the app becomes unusable.
findings.relevance: A learning knowledge graph for one student unlikely to exceed 200-500 nodes. But if supporting multi-subject curricula or shared graphs, could approach limits.

findings.detail: Performance drops when panning or zooming with many elements. Custom nodes with complex React components exacerbate this.
findings.relevance: Canvas interaction smoothness is critical for learning UX. Must use React.memo, useCallback, and minimize node complexity.

findings.detail: Components depending on the nodes array re-render on any node change. Viewport changes trigger state updates that cascade re-renders.
findings.relevance: Solvable with proper memoization patterns. Well-documented optimization guide exists.

reactflow_limitations.mitigation_assessment: For a learning graph (typically 50-300 nodes), ReactFlow is well within safe performance range. Key mitigations: React.memo on custom nodes, useMemo/useCallback for props, separate selection state. If future scaling demands arise, ReactFlow can be replaced with a canvas-based renderer while keeping the same data model.

shadcn_ui_desktop_issues.summary: shadcn/ui works well in Tauri desktop apps. Main concern is cross-platform WebView CSS variable differences, which is a Tauri issue rather than shadcn-specific.

findings.issue: Tauri WebView CSS inconsistencies affect shadcn components
findings.detail: Since Tauri uses different WebView engines per OS, some CSS features may render differently. shadcn/ui relies on Tailwind CSS which is generally well-supported, but edge cases exist.
findings.relevance: Test shadcn components on all target platforms. Use standard CSS features only.

findings.issue: CLI detection issue with Vite projects
findings.detail: shadcn CLI may not recognize Vite projects initially. Config file must be named vite.config.js for proper detection.

shadcn_ui_desktop_issues.mitigation_assessment: shadcn/ui is a safe choice. The tauri-ui template (github.com/agmmnn/tauri-ui) provides a proven starting point for Tauri + shadcn/ui integration.

tauri_shell_plugin_issues.summary: The Tauri shell plugin has multiple documented issues specifically relevant to our sidecar CLI integration pattern.

findings.detail: Intermittent, production-only, Windows-specific. Root cause unclear.
kill() completes but process continues running. Affects PyInstaller binaries and general subprocesses.

findings.issue: Scope configuration errors despite correct config
findings.detail: Shell scope validation is overly strict or buggy. Programs listed in config still get rejected.
Feature request for proper sidecar lifecycle management (health checks, restart, graceful shutdown) is open but unimplemented.

tauri_shell_plugin_issues.mitigation_assessment: This is the highest-risk area. Recommended mitigations: (1) Use localhost HTTP/WebSocket for IPC instead of stdin/stdout to avoid spawn hanging. (2) Implement custom Rust-side process group killing for Windows. (3) Add health check polling for sidecar liveness. (4) Consider running CLI tool as a separate long-lived service rather than spawning per-interaction.

svelte_in_tauri.evidence_summary: Svelte + Tauri 2 is now functional and stable for basic apps. SvelteKit integration is officially documented. However, issues remain: (1) zbus compilation failures on fresh projects (May 2025), (2) Linux build failures on Arch. These are platform-specific, not fundamental. The original rejection reason (compilation instability) has been PARTIALLY resolved - basic setups work, but edge cases persist.
svelte_in_tauri.recommendation: Svelte in Tauri is now viable but offers no compelling advantage over React for this project. React has larger ecosystem, more ReactFlow integration examples, and better shadcn/ui support. No reason to switch.

electron_for_education.evidence_summary: Electron's advantages over Tauri are: (1) consistent Chromium rendering across all platforms, (2) larger plugin ecosystem, (3) JavaScript-only backend (no Rust needed), (4) more mature multi-window support. However, none of these are education-specific advantages. No evidence found that Electron has unique educational app capabilities that Tauri cannot match. The performance and bundle size advantages of Tauri are more relevant for a learning app that must feel lightweight and responsive.
electron_for_education.recommendation: Electron remains a valid choice but offers no education-specific advantage. Tauri's smaller bundle size (8MB vs 130MB) and lower memory usage are better for a learning tool that runs alongside other apps.

custom_whiteboard_not_reactflow.evidence_summary: Alternative canvas libraries assessed: (1) tldraw SDK 4.0 - excellent infinite canvas but designed for whiteboard/drawing, not node-based knowledge graphs. Requires paid license for production. (2) Cytoscape.js - powerful graph library with built-in layouts but Canvas-based (bypasses React virtual DOM). (3) D3.js force graph - maximum flexibility but massive implementation effort. (4) JsPlumb - positioned as ReactFlow alternative but less community traction. None offer a better fit than ReactFlow for a node-based learning knowledge graph with React.

detail.tldraw: Good SDK but wrong use case (drawing/whiteboard, not structured node graph). Production license required. Would need significant custom work to make it behave like a knowledge graph editor.
detail.cytoscape: Better for pure graph visualization with auto-layout. But Canvas-based rendering breaks React paradigm. Better suited for read-only graph display than interactive editing.
detail.d3_force: Maximum flexibility but enormous implementation cost. Every primitive must be built from scratch.
detail.jsplumb: Claims to be ReactFlow alternative but smaller community, less documentation.

custom_whiteboard_not_reactflow.recommendation: ReactFlow remains the best fit. It is purpose-built for interactive node-based UIs in React, has 19k+ GitHub stars, 400k+ weekly npm downloads, MIT license, and extensive documentation. The ~1000 node ceiling is acceptable for learning graphs.

top_risks_ranked.risk: Tauri Shell Plugin - Windows process management
top_risks_ranked.impact: Core feature (CLI sidecar) may hang or leave orphan processes on Windows
top_risks_ranked.mitigation: Use HTTP/WebSocket IPC instead of stdin/stdout. Implement Rust-side process group killing. Add health check polling.

top_risks_ranked.impact: UI may look/behave differently on Windows vs macOS vs Linux
top_risks_ranked.mitigation: Use standard CSS features, test on all platforms, use CSS variables, avoid WebKit-specific features.

top_risks_ranked.risk: ReactFlow performance with growing knowledge graphs
top_risks_ranked.impact: Canvas becomes laggy if graph exceeds ~500 complex nodes
top_risks_ranked.mitigation: React.memo on custom nodes, lazy loading, virtualization. Can migrate to canvas-based renderer if needed.

top_risks_ranked.risk: Tauri ecosystem maturity - fewer plugins and community resources
top_risks_ranked.impact: May need custom Rust code for features that Electron has plugins for
top_risks_ranked.mitigation: Evaluate all needed native features upfront. Use Tauri's official plugin set. Write minimal Rust for gaps.

top_risks_ranked.impact: App won't start for users with this Windows 11 feature enabled
top_risks_ranked.mitigation: Monitor Tauri issue tracker. This affects a small user subset on Insider builds.

overall_risk_assessment.strongest_counter_argument: The Tauri shell plugin's Windows subprocess management issues (hanging spawn, unkillable processes) are the strongest argument against this stack. Since the core product depends on spawning and managing a CLI sidecar (claude-code), these are not theoretical risks but documented production bugs. The mitigation (HTTP/WebSocket IPC) adds architectural complexity but is proven in other Tauri apps.
overall_risk_assessment.recommendation: The Tauri+React+ReactFlow stack is viable but the sidecar integration layer needs careful architectural design from day 1. The shell plugin issues should NOT be a reason to switch stacks (Electron has its own process management quirks), but they DO require a robust IPC strategy that does not rely solely on stdin/stdout.
